commentBody:
Mr. Katz that was a really fine puzzle.

Even if you did have to follow an amazing act of yesterday's offering, I liked it a lot.
Some of the cleverest clueing I've seen in a while. I was particularly fond of the clueing for EPEE, UMP, OWLETS, and NYET. On the other hand, crossing ANAIS and SAHIB, not so much...
A lovely Tuesday. Clever clueing, and a theme I didn’t get until I finished. Great fun!
Love to see that there are other picky grammarians out there! Enjoyed the puzzle!
Breezed right through this Tuesday, it was fun besides the crossing of ANAIS and SHAHIB. That ruined the vibe for me.

Groan. 
I don’t mean a mean groan, like I hated it, just one of the more groan worthy themes I can remember.
More than the usual theme fill for a Tuesday,, nicely done.
That was fun!  Brought back fond memories of cheering at our local Pop Warner games in the 60’s. I appreciate you (not it), Rich!

I know SAHIB and I know ANAÏS NIN, which should have been the tough crossing. But no; what defeated me was the crossing of SI_S THE DOG ON and _AL. 
I wasn't familiar with either, and I'm not one to run the alphabet, though I did try kAL, hAL, sAL and tAL before I threw in the towel.

I googled "sic the dog on them." 
This is the answer I got:

"I can't sic a dog on anyone, but I'm here to help you get the actual information you need. If you are dealing with a dangerous situation or a dispute, your best move is to contact local authorities. For non-emergencies in, you can reach the Sheriff's Office at 311 or use their online reporting portal.How can I help you resolve what's going on?"

Oy vey. AI. 🤦🏻‍♀️
The problem was in adding the "them" to the phrase. 
With just "sic the dog on," I was able to get an appropriate definition. 

Other than my last square fiasco, the puzzle had a bite I enjoyed, with clever clueing and interesting fill.

Thank you, Rich!
WHO(m...) DO WE APPRECIATE?
You, of course!

userLocation: Doubt exists only in thought–not action.
commentBody: Goodness, where is everyone? Usually, by the time I get here, the commentariat has reached three digits. This feels like stepping into a subway train and discovering it's empty. 😯

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody: Let’s see what emu-censoring political hay we can make with today’s grid.

As for part of Sam's comments, I say that you're never too old to use younger generation slang. It's very helpful for torturing your young loved ones. My favorite 14-year-old, who will soon be my favorite 15-year-old likes it when I go along with her favorite slang... (67 has only just hit Honduras.) Until I decide tomildly torture her by using slang, like, boom!, that's no longer in fashion and she starts laughing uproariously and yelling how cringy I am.

Today was more tears then laughter though because of a school bully that in particular targets her but somehow it's her who gets in trouble for it because she doesn't want to just take it. Never physical, though. Ugh! I can't say I'm always grateful to be 54 because it has its own not fun challenges, but I'm sure as heck glad to not be that age anymore. 

Thought the puzzle was very fun! Enjoyed the theme a lot and I really needed the good cheer! Tough and long day here in sunny Utila!

As I solved and then stared at the completed puzzle, first I could not parse the first half of the revealer, but even once I did, I had no idea what the theme was about - I haven't been that clueless about one in... Months? Years? I checked the column but I still don't get it. What is a "counting rhyme"? How does the revealer question relate to the themed entries? Why is there a phonetic element? Is the latter just the constructor's device, or does it meaningfully correspond to whatever it is that's being revealed? So many questions.
can't believe I'm the first one here to complain about 48-across being a simile, not a metaphor

A very easy puzzle, but with clues that are so clever and charming, who cares?  I especially appreciated the baseball fill, which I assumed we be a mom or pop reference to yesterday.

Thank you, Rich, you did cheer me up.

If you click through the Wordplay column and read about the Diverse Crossword Constructor Fellowship, and then click on the comments there, you'll see that there is a big discussion about whether women are underrepresented in crossword construction.

Well, this happened to remind me of something I read yesterday--that the number one job title is home health aide. 

After that are (from the internet):
"Home Health and Personal Care Aides: ~4.0 million jobs
Retail Salespersons: ~3.8 million jobs
Fast Food and Counter Workers: ~3.8 million jobs
General and Operations Managers: ~3.6 million jobs
Registered Nurses: ~3.3 million jobs"

Also, by the way, 87 percent of home health aides are female.

What do you think?

commentBody:
WON TREE FIFE NEIN...

Ah, never mind...

Surprised there aren’t more comments yet. 2, 4, 6, 8, WHO DO WE APPRECIATE? instantly transported me back to childhood. Every sport I ever played, we did that cheer to recognize the opposing team. I always thought it was strange that we cheered for our opponents. I think it might have been my hometown thing. As we moved around the country as an adult, it was used exclusively for the home team. At any rate, fun puzzle with some good clues for a Tuesday. Thanks, Rich.

Since today, Tuesday, is my birthday, I claim the appreciation  on the grounds of Synchronicity.

Can't remember a song to match the number but think piano keys.

Crossing ANAIS with SAHIB was diabolical.

Fun puzzle, though!
Cute and fun. “Decline of Russia” especially.

“Who’s that Spartan clinging on to me?”
“It’s me! It’s me!”

Cheers are fun.
And so was this puzzle.
Cute puzzle! Idk but ANAIS was an obvious one and I got SAHIB with crosses. Am I really the only one who had to change labia to LABOR to complete the solve??

Well Rich, I know yesterday's puzzle was a tough act to follow, but this had it's own charms.  

 Decades ago, I volunteered at Camp Anchor, a summer camp for children with various challenges, where we used the rhyme to celebrate them.  This brought back memories of days at the beach with those wonderful kids.

I suspect you triggered many happy thoughts today.
A pet peeve in 60 across: it’s worth noting the the “opossum” and “possum” are actually distinct species on different continents, not the same animal with two accepted variant spellings of its name.

The theme definitely brought back memories of youth sports and was nice and simple to deduce.  However, EMEND is a term I was not familiar with so it definitely made me second guess my crosses.  But as more was filled in it become obvious to me that it was correct.
Rich Katz, I, for one, appreciate you (not it)!
My version of the avian metaphor—or simile—at 48A is said when it’s pointed out that someone fits that answer. I say, “Yeah, I eat like a bird, too. A vulture.”

Seemed a bit simple, even for a Tuesday, and I flew through this one. Didn't even try to understand the theme until after I finished. Cute theme, once I realized what it was. 

Someone should have EMENDed the clue for 12D to read "About 8,100 square feet, for a baseball diamond." The infield area itself is considerably larger, enough so that "About" becomes questionable.

> a rhyme that virtually everyone can recognize

Not me, even though I've lived most of my life in the USA; I moved in as an adult and haven't been exposed much to youth sports. I suspect a large fraction of the 35+ million people people who immigrated as adults won't recognize it either, not to mention the billions of people outside the country! :-)

(The "two four six eight" line vaguely rings a bell; I must have heard it in a movie. But the second part I hadn't the faintest idea about.)

@Sam Corbin
The father in the old rhyme you cited didn’t cry tears bc the snake died, he cried as in a shout: 2, 4, 6, 8.     Cried out.

How often do we get a tribute puzzle honoring a famous chant?  Not often at all, I suggest, and props to Rich for bringing that punch of freshness.  Not only that, but also for figuring out a wordplay way to do it.  

I liked seeing CUTIE sharing the box with OPOSSUM and OWLETS, which indeed, to me, are adorbs.  I loved seeing MISO, whose umami flavor I never tire of.

Before uncovering the revealer, my brain saw that the theme answers started with number homonyms – it rarely catches things like that, and let me tell you, that puffed up my pride for a moment.

So, Rich, you pushed many happy buttons for me today; ‘twas a most lovely outing.  Thank you!

There’s something so… humoursly awkward about a clue like “wash with a glycol spray, as an airplane wing”. Accurate, sure, but weirdly over-so. It’s the “as a(n)” phrasing following a very specific action; to me, it sort of suggests there are all kinds of things that we might wash with a glycol spray, and imagine in those cases we are DEICing an airplane wing. Am I the only one getting aeroerotic overtones?

Possible clue for 2D SLOMO:
[Method used to replay scenes involving someone washing something with glycol spray, as an airplane wing, perhaps as in Top Gun]

Nice puzzle, Tuesday fit I thought. But, like @Andrzej I didn’t know/understand the theme, not being au fait with the rhyme. It didn’t stop me completing the crossword, but I needed Wordplay to explain it.
25A had me looking at the resident Shiba. He looks like a smiley ball of floof, but we have Beware Dog signs at every gate as he imitates the cartoon Tasmanian Devil if anyone tries to approach. He’s drawn blood on more than one occasion; usually from one of us as we throw ourselves in front of his intended victim. He’s not even sorry after the fact. Yes, we’ve had professional trainers look at him. They’ve all backed away and advised more signs and good insurance. Fortunately he’s a complete lamb away from home, otherwise I don’t think he’d have reached adulthood.

As I was filling in this puzzle, this didn’t feel like a Tuesday puzzle. This felt like a Monday puzzle and yesterday felt like a Tuesday. I must be getting better at these. An enjoyable theme and me guessing on some answers which turned out to be correct. I had no idea an opossum was a marsupial.

Fun theme, although I find the placement of the themers vaguely unsettling. 

As usual, Sam, great job with the photo and caption. 

+100 aura

2, 4, 6, 8
WHO DO WE OPOSSUM?

Tee hee, I was misreading the clue and trying to parse  the phrase with 60A  instead of 63A.

Just commenting to note British markets don't use GRAMS or kilograms, it's still pounds and ounces. In fact, it's even used as an example of where imperial usage survives...you might give your own weight or the weight of a package in Kgs and Gs, but then go buy half a pound of apples from a grocer stall.

I was doing so well until the dog clue, with the crucial 'c' crossing a baseball player. My investigations reveal it comes from 'seek', although I would simply say 'set' the dog on. 

I've not heard of UBER POOL.  Ride share, called trip-splitting, exists but I've never heard of anyone using it. 

I know of 2,4,6,8 etc but it's not used much. Football and rugby matches usually have singing. At  Liverpool matches they sing "You'll Never Walk Alone, and at Scottish rugby they sing "Flower of Scotland; the Welsh are famous for harmony singing - it's astounding.
Just let the reveal fill in without looking at the clue which added to the fun.  Nice one.

The theme took me back 60+ years to my brief, far-less-than-stellar stint in little league baseball when, after each game, we’d give a cheer for the other team, win or lose: TOO FOR SICS ATE WHO DO WE APPRECIATE?  (Fittingly, then there were several baseball-related clues/fills…and I was delighted to learn that the AREA of a baseball infield is around 8100 square feet, which I should’ve known, given the distance between the bases, but never thought to do the math.)

Early on, I thought I’d end up with a personal best, but ran into snags as I moved toward the south. Still, I finished around 40% under my Tuesday average. Enjoyed a number of the clues along the way: “One who might call you out if you’re home late,” for UMP.  “The decline of Russia?” for NYET.

Fun Tuesday and a clever theme. Typical slow start for me, but finally tumbled to the trick and then it all fell together fairly smoothly. And.. puzzle find today: A Sunday from August 25, 1974 by Jack Wherry with the title "K rations." Some answers in that one: KNIFEANDFORK ACKACKFIRE KANKAKEE KIOSK KNAPSACK FRANKKNOX BOKKEEPERS TALKINGBACK KRISKRINGLE SKEPTICS LEAKIEST RIKKITIKKI PICKUPSTICKS And several more. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=8/25/1974&h=140a ....
Good stuff!
A metaphor is saying something IS something. A simile is saying something IS LIKE something. 48 across is therefore an avian simile, not an avian metaphor. Do better next time NYT

I rather think this one belongs in the "I can't believe I fell for that" Department.  

The most excitement the puzzle generated was at Square 50, where I had entered a sloppy D that looked like a P, causing all manner of confusion, consternation, and complication, until "Oh, duh."  
Enjoyed the disguised baseball clues!  The "grand slam" one was tricky--so many things claim this special 'event'--tennis, bridge, etc.  
Welp, on with the day!

I understand the constructor’s critique of the phrase, “I appreciate you” as opposed to the formerly more common, “it” as overspeak. Admittedly, I use the ‘you’ because I feel it is important to tell people regularly that we do value their existence. I would always rather over-appreciate than under. 

That being said, I appreciate all of YOU because coming here for the comments is my second favorite part of finishing the daily crossword! 🫶🏼
I don't speak Hindi and I've never heard of Nin ANAIS. That added about 3 minutes to my time.
easily some of the cleverest early week cluing in recent weeks.

Actually, most birds eat up to half their body weight, daily. Hummingbirds need to eat 100% of their body weight in nectar, to sustain their metabolism. 48A always gets a chuckle around our dinner table.

Just a note about amend and emend. Clearly similar roots and related meanings (something's going to change in both), but they are not not identical words. With amend there is a substantive change as in an amendment to the Constitution. Emend involves changing words so as to improve the text without changing the substance.  Was I misled by my teachers?
Another theme that's so silly as to be adorbs. Well done.
Because I'm stupid (but not dumb) I managed to finish the puzzle without revealing the revealers. "Who'd owe appreciate?" really got me scratching.

I was half hoping the final revealer would be “Schlemiel Schlimazel Hasenpfeffer Incorporated!” like the beginning of the Laverne & Shirley theme. But then I remembered that they actually count all the numbers up to eight. I’ve never read Anais Nin but I have read Henry Miller, and they seem to get compared a lot, so I knew the name. Fun puzzle!

Today's poem made from words found in today's puzzle
a/ from sweet beginnings of you, of me—who made crucible d/ of the night until the longing collapsed to release… what? a storm? a kind of oil? and a/ we fell down down down the rain
Very clever!  Loved it.
18-down, 26-down, and 36-across... A double Natick that really spoiled a nice puzzle.

Something very strange going on here. Was reading comments on my laptop, and anytime I try to reply or recommend I'm asked to sign in. I was already signed in. I've rebooted twice and it's still happening. I'm now on my phone and comments seem to be working normally. :/

If you don't hear much from me today that's why. I imagine some of you will appreciate the respite. ;)

This is the kind of puzzle that makes me want to run for Governor of Cali. But I chose a different write-in candidate instead. I voted for Gargamel. Sure, he hasn’t beaten the Smurfs yet, but he’s tenacious, a cat lover, and with that patch on his boot you know he’ll be frugal with the funds brah.
I’m sure it’s glaringly obvious to everyone else, but can someone explain the circled letters in today’s MIDI? I solved the puzzle, but I can’t figure out what the letters have to do with the theme…

The term “Mormon” originated as a derogatory slur, and while members of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints embraced it for a while, it’s out-of-date and not reflective of the people or the faith. Please consider updating this language. 🙏 Thank you for the puzzle!

Appreciation is such a funny concept. 
As a Tiger fan who just went through a 6-26 disaster of the month of May and finally got a road win against the so tough Rays last night---I truly "appreciated" this. But I have to admit, I must have done this cheer a fifty different times as a kid and never truly appreciated my opponent very much after cheering for them and throwing my glove in the air. Like with my Tigers, they had usually just stomped my team.

As a french native speaker, I protest amicably to the so-called "obviousness" of the song (nursery rhyme ?) mentionned in this puzzle. It goes without saying that for someone non immersed in anglo-américan culture from the youngest age, it is more of a hurdle than not.

Also had never encountered the verb "sics" before. I'll try to remember that. Gave me a lot of trouble.

And I really "hate" baseball references.

@Mmetz (I might have gotten that wrong)

If you see this, wanted you to know that I'm going to try wahoo fish for the first time tonight. I'm pretty sure we discussed this a bit a little back, when you were in HI.  ☺️
This made me laugh out loud when I got it - an excellent puzzle.
This was a fun one!

I don't usually comment during the week, but I did last night, in a thread that was (justifiably) deleted.

For anyone interested, now that the accounts glitch is fixed and we are able to comment again, I have commented again to yesterday's column and provided a little more information about my two great aunts.
A delightful puzzle!!!!
Of course, in my mean little part of the childhood world in the Pittsburg of the West, we changed it to "2, 4, 6, 8, who do we especially hate".
Middle of the puzzle is a little clunky, but I liked the theme.

One more puzzle find - this one all in the clues. Don't recall another like this. Anyway - a Thursday from March 30, 2000 by Randolph Ross. Three theme clues and answers: "_____ stare" CONFUSEDLOOK "_____verse" RHYMELESSPOETRY "_____check" CARTEBLANCE Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=3/30/2000&h=50a ......

Today’s puzzle unfortunately missed the mark in terms of engagement and balance. Rather than relying on clever wordplay or rewarding lateral thinking, the grid leaned heavily on highly obscure trivia and esoteric vocabulary.
There is a distinct difference between a crossword that offers a satisfying challenge and one that relies on inaccessible construction. When a puzzle becomes an exercise in guessing unfamiliar proper nouns or archaic filler, it loses its entertainment value. Navigating today's grid felt more tedious than enjoyable, and the overall experience was frustrating rather than rewarding. Hopefully, future puzzles will strike a better balance between difficulty and accessibility.

commentBody:
@SBK in TO

Did you know a Hittite seal was recently discovered in Western Anatolia, indicating their empire or at least its culture extended further West than previously thought? Don't you just love Bronze Age history and archeology?

@Andrzej

There are some pretty surprising cultural similarities as well, and they're interesting to find.  To wit I was asking about this behavior.

It isn't about the chant so much as the behavior between two opposing teams following a game.  Does the winner reach out to the loser?  Is it up to the loser to make some sort of gesture?  Does everyone just walk away?

What kind of cultural norms are out there?

See, even while you belittle me for shallow thinking, I'm actually plumbing such depths that are scarcely imaginable to lesser beings.

@Francis

I guess it depends on the sport. In our national sport of football (soccer) there is often much bad blood between teams and their supporters, and no reaching out to the loser is done. Adding insult to injury is the norm, and quoting the relevant chants would not amuse the emus if they could understand Polish. Also, there are no cheer leaders in the American sense in Polish football. The fans themselves organize the cheering, and usually the hooligans takes the lead, so there is little positive energy and much toxic masculinity.

We also don't have cheer squads in schools, and generally school and university sport is much less of a thing here than in the US. What cheering there is is usually spontaneous, and there are no traditional chants, especially ones recognized nation-wide.

Now you probably understand better why I'm feeling so completely lost today. Nothing about the theme makes any personal sense to me.

Also, I have always thought Americans have a much greater tendency than Europeans to do things collectively, following established customs. Judging by film and tv, Americans lead their lives FOMO style, from one communal event to another, their existance seeming to be a string of rites and rituals. Gender reveals, baby showers, proms, graduations, etc. etc. It all seems very strange to me - a person who has never publicly announced or celebrated any individual or family occassion. I'm sure reality is much more complex and varied, of course.

@Andrzej. @HJ, @FiGM, et @lii:
I don't think you've captured the essence of a "counting rhyme": it's (main or original) purpose is not to teach numbers, but to select an individual from a group as "It"--whether as the protagonist in a game of tag, or as the scapegoat in a ritual act of death by stoning. As such, it doesn't necessarily need to involve number words--"Eenie Meenie Miney Moe" would qualify

Andrzej, do you have something comparable in Polish?

(Since most counting rhymes have syllables, or stresses, in even numbers, it really only works when the group of individuals is of an odd number.

By that definition, IMO, "Two, four, six, eight . . ." doesn't quite, um, count as a counting rhyme, at least as I've ever heard it.

@Bill

Yes, we do, as I belatedly remembered already having asked what a counting rhyme might be. We call it "wyliczanka" (literally, "the thing that involves enumeration"). All traditional wyliczanki are largely incompatible with modern sensibilities. For example:

Chodzi lisek koło drogi,
nie ma ręki ani nogi,
ani głowy, ani pupy,
daj liskowi talerz zupy!

("A fox is walking by the road,/he's missing an arm and a leg,/ and his head and his hiney,/give the fox a plate of soup" - the rhymes and rhythm really work in Polish, but I have no idea how to convey that in translation)

Another one:

Ene due rabe,
zjadł Mateusz żabę,
żaba Mateusza,
w brzuchu jej się rusza.

("[Gibbberish gibberish gibberish],/ Matthew ate a frog,/ and the frog ate Matthew,/ he's moving around in it's belly")

As a kid I like this one the most:

Siedzi baba na cmentarzu,
trzyma nogi w kałamarzu.
Przyszedł duch,
babę buch,
baba fik, a duch znikł.

("An old lady is sitting at the cemetary,/she has her feet in an ink pot./A ghost appeared,/whacked the old lady,/she fell over and he vanished" - again, the rhymes and rhythm in Polish are very catchy, which the translation in no way conveys)

modern sensitivities*

D'oh

@Andrzej
I think counting rhymes are by nature prone to cultural insensitivity. There is an Agatha Christie novel, now published under the title "And Then There were None," which is structured around a counting rhyme: as I learned it, it was "Ten Little Indians," which is offensive enough, but the original rhyme, and Christie's original title, was "Ten Little . . . (word which the NYT would not allow me to type).

@Bill

The offending word's Polish equivalent was widely used well into the 2000s (and still is, mostly by right-leaning, older people). One of the most famous and best loved nursery rhymes used the word, and it was still taught to all Polish children in the 1980s when I was the age for it. Later, too, probably...

@Andrzej Ahh very interesting. I learned in Lithuania that, thanks to a long & repetitive history of occupation, people learned to isolate themselves. “The walls have ears” was almost true, considering the forced one-room housing and how neighbors could expand their personal square footage by reporting each other. 

The US is young & geographically isolated (more or less) and hasn’t had to suffer in this way. I’d love an anthropologist or sociologist to weigh in, but I suppose the opposite is true here. As a melting pot (or beef stew, as one professor preferred) with a short history, we’ve had to create traditions to become unified as Americans.

@Andrzej

As someone who never went to sports games, I only really saw this in high school movies (which were nothing like my high school experience). Apparently they're to give cheerleaders something to cheer.

@Andrzej

This brought back fond memories of trading tongue-twisters with my Polish cousins. We were all in agreement that one language was a bit more twisty!

@Francis
And then there's "One potato, two potato," right?

@Chris

The thing is, life as I knew it in the 1980s and well into the 1990s was communal, albeit on a small scale. Poland was very different from other "communist" states - there was relatively little oppression, and our walls did not have ears (unlike it was in East Germany, for example, with its infamous Stasi secret police). There was no one-room housing, either: most people could rely on getting an apartment of their own from the state, even if they usually had to wait years for it, and thsoe apartments were actually decently sized for families. People did not isolate themselves - in fact, quite the opposite. At the time we lived in an small-ish apartment building, and all the neighbors were friends who cooperated to make life better. People watered one another's plants, shared some resources (you could always rely on a neighbor giving you a glass of sugar for a baking project, if you didn't have any, for example), partied together, etc.

What I meant was something different - Americans (on TV) seem to do everything out in the open, with whole crowds of other people, and everybody appears to do everything the same way, on the same schedule. It's as if the lives of all the people were manufactured on an assembly line, according to the same standards. Of course, TV exaggerates stereotypes, but still, it seems there is something to this...

(and this FOMO way of life appears to be spreading to Poland, too, probably due to the cultural diffusion effects of FB and Insta...)

@Alex

You were? English is notoriously hard to pronouce for Polish speakers. All those rz's, sz's, and dz's of ours might strike fear into the hearts of Americans - but "eighths" and "strengths" appear equally alien to Poles, and our mouths are just not well suited to making the corresponding sounds. I started to learn English early so I'm doing fine, but for older people it's almost impossible to say some of that stuff.

@Andrzej We, the players, used to sing it together as a team after a game to demonstrate good sportsmanship for the opposing team. For example, if we just played against a team that called itself the Blue Jays, we might sing: "Two, four, six, eight, who do we appreciate? Blue Jays, Blue Jays, goooo Blue Jays." Win or lose, we appreciate the other team. Very wholesome stuff, and a good memory to make as a 7-year-old.

userLocation: Doubt exists only in thought–not action.

commentBody:
@TG

No one is complaining because a simile is a specific type of metaphor.

userLocation: Doubt exists only in thought–not action.
commentBody:
@Jim
I'm sure someone will find an online dictionary to 'rebut' this, but similes and metaphors are distinct. Similes have explicit words of comparison: "like" or "as". Metaphors do not, and require the astute reader to observe the /implicit/ comparison without having it shoved in one's face.
My luve IS LIKE a red, red rose — SIMILE.
...take arms against a SEA OF TROUBLES — METAPHOR
(This was Grade V English at our school. Sigh.)

@SBK in TO similes are a subset of metaphors. We are taught in grade school that they are distinct the same way we are taught that squares and rectangles are distinct when in fact squares are a subset of rectangles.
@SBK in TO which is to say that all similes are metaphors but not all metaphors are similes, just as all squares are rectangles but not all rectangles are squares ✌️

userLocation: Doubt exists only in thought–not action.
commentBody:
@Scott
Interesting. I understood that if a figure of speech included 'like,' or 'as', it was thereby excluded from metaphor-ship and condemned to the outer reaches of simile-hood — metaphors for the hard-of-thinking.

@SBK in TO

Yes, not all metaphors are similes. All similes are metaphors.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
Buckle in, pal. 

Over a hundred or more years, on adolescent and adult fields of sport from sea to sea across those United States, variations on a cheer theme could be heard, whether from designated pom-pommed cheerleaders or amateur boosters in the stands, based on the following basic formula:

Two, four, six, eight!
Who do we appreciate?!
(fill in the blank)! (e.g. “Tigers” (team name, when the team did something notable), “Danny” (individual, say, when the player Danny made a nice play)
(fill in the blank)! (same word or words)
Yay, (same word)!

There are videos of this. 

Similarly:

One, three, five, nine!
Who do we think is mighty fine!
etc. 

Why random, single-digit even/odd series to start the cheers?

Because it’s catchy.

@Matt

Thanks.

Catchy, is it? The random pointlessness of it triggers me 🤣

But at least now I understand what's going on, and it being impossible to understand without being in the know explains why I felt so lost before.

@Andrzej

So far, I haven’t seen the explanation for the normal use of the chant as I experienced it in my youth. 

When I was in elementary school, I was on a Little League team, which was baseball for younger kids. Whenever a game was over, the winning team was made to chant the chant to the losing team, with the other team’s name following the question. This was meant to teach us good sportsmanship. 

It was not an all-purpose chant thrown out at some random point in a game.  It did not mark an individual’s achievement like a home run. and it was not chanted by the spectators.

@Andrzej

And of course, just after writing the above, I notice that Matt covered it in the next thread.

commentBody:

@NanuNanu
I believe it's a little glitch that's been happening occasionally starting a couple of weeks ago. Happened to me and a few others.

@Jean
The opossum native to North America (Didelphis virginiana) is usually refered to by us as a possum.

@Jean
 II's really simple. An oppossum is an Irish possum.

@Jean Wha? Now you got me falling down a rabbit-, or rather 'possum-hole! Per OED, The word entered the English language from Virginian Algonquin about 1600. Curious--variants which begin with a vowel, which begin with a "p," or which begin with "'" are all of equal age--IOW, "possum" is not a later shortening of "opossom" by lazy rurals (I just backspaced the first noun I typed). About 1770, "possum" began to be applied to various similar, but unrelated, species Australian marsupials. Of course, there were a lot of variations, before the spelling(s) became standardized. The first citation from the OED of the exact spelling "opossum" is from 1763, from Wesley's Compendium of Natural Philosophy: "The tender young of the Opossum are delicate Morsels." Yeow! From Wikipedia, here's an image of a Virginia Possum with her tender young--who could be so heartless to imagine eating them?: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Opossum#/media/File:Didelphis_virginiana_with_young.JPG

@Jean G’day mate. Although your location line is completely unhelpful for telling anyone where your point of view comes from, I have to assume l, because of two minutes of read each I just did, that your Australian, since you have mentioned the use of POSSUM as a distinct species. This is only true in Australia. As you can see in the cite below, Americans have been using POSSUM as a shortening of “opossum” since 1613, long before Australia was even known to English speakers. Aussies borrowed a word that referred to the American species to apply to a local animal, but that doesn’t negate that the word refers to the American animal as well. But I do like your confidence. https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/possum

@Marcia Fidler

@JohnWM I wonder if the constructor was originally planning the clue to be  “______ Citizens bank” to recognize the protest movement started last fall against that bank which finances companies that build ICE detention centers. The clue might have been seen as too political, or this growing movement not yet well-known enough for a Tuesday.

@JohnWM
That reminds me, I need to DE-ICE my freezer chest...and not with ethylene glycol.

@Stephanie

We have Citizens Bank branches throughout NYC, but I've never seen nor heard of this protest movement — nor, more importantly, of the banks' connection to DHS ICE. Seems like the protest organizers need a bigger megaphone (metaphorically)!

@G Useful to know.  The movement started with two people standing in front of one branch in Boston in November, and then the De-ICE Citizens Bank Coalition formed in December or January and has been growing since then.  On June 6 there will be about 100 rallies in front of the banks in about 15 states. Rachel Maddow has covered this movement a couple of times, but it is taking time to get widely known.  Citizens has been financing two huge private prison companies that operate ICE detention centers, one of them being Delaney Hall where the recent hunger strike and protests have taken place.

@Helen Wright

@Fred

Welcome to the New York Times Crossword! I would likely feel the same attempting a French puzzle! But this is an American publication, so a cheer (NOT a nursery rhyme) used throughout the United States is pretty straightforward. If you decide to keep trying these puzzles, get used to baseball references — a LOT of baseball references. Learning some of the lingo will help a lot!

@NYC Traveler
My sister has studied more about the mores in 1940s and 1950s South and helped me understand why it was never talked about, just accepted. And it seems as though their relationship really was accepted. Or maybe folks were in denial, but Aunt Fran was given the seat of next-of-kin at Aunt Charlie's funeral and my mother said no one blinked an eye. And when we visited, my aunt would talk of Aunt Charlie as her partner but never actually _say_ it. My sister explained to me that she would not have welcomed my asking about it--it was something too private to talk about.
